theorem_statement:
Glassey blow-up argument for the focusing mass-critical nonlinear Schrodinger equation.

Consider a sufficiently regular solution \(u(t,x)\) to the focusing mass-critical NLS on \(\mathbb{R}^d\):

\[
i\partial_t u+\Delta u=-|u|^{4/d}u.
\]

Assume:

- \(u_0\in H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- \(|x|u_0\in L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- the energy \(E(u_0)\) is negative, where

\[
E(u)=\frac12\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|\nabla u|^2\,dx
-\frac{1}{2+4/d}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]

Then the maximal forward existence time \(T\) is finite and

\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as }t\uparrow T.
\]

nodes.title: Finite-time blow-up from negative energy
nodes.informal_statement: For a sufficiently regular solution \(u\) of \(i\partial_t u+\Delta u=-|u|^{4/d}u\) with \(u_0\in H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)\), \(|x|u_0\in L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)\), and \(E(u_0)<0\), the maximal forward existence time \(T\) is finite and \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Define the variance \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\). By the virial identity in the mass-critical case, \(V''(t)=16E(u(t))\). Energy conservation gives \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), so \(V\) is a concave quadratic with negative second derivative. Since \(V(t)\ge 0\) by definition, it cannot stay nonnegative for all large positive times; hence there is a finite forward time \(T\) with \(V(T)=0\) and thus \(V(t)	o 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\). Using the weighted inequality \(\|f\|_{L^2}^2\le  rac{2}{d}\|xf\|_{L^2}\|
abla f\|_{L^2}\) with \(f=u(t)\), together with mass conservation \(\|u(t)\|_{L^2}=\|u_0\|_{L^2}=:M>0\), we obtain \(M^2\le  rac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|
abla u(t)\|_{L^2}\). As \(t\uparrow T\), the factor \(\sqrt{V(t)}	o 0\) while \(M>0\) is fixed, so necessarily \(\|
abla u(t)\|_{L^2}	o\infty\). Since \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}^2=\|u(t)\|_{L^2}^2+\|
abla u(t)\|_{L^2}^2\) and the \(L^2\) norm is conserved, \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}	o\infty\). Therefore the solution blows up in finite positive time.

nodes.title: Virial identity for the variance
nodes.informal_statement:
For \(V(t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\), one has
\[
V'(t)=4\operatorname{Im}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}x\cdot \nabla u(t,x)\,\overline{u(t,x)}\,dx
\]
and, in the focusing mass-critical case,
\[
V''(t)=16E(u(t)).
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Differentiate \(V(t)\) once:
\[
V'(t)=2\operatorname{Re}\int |x|^2u_t\overline u\,dx.
\]
Using \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\), the nonlinear term drops out because \(\int |x|^2|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx\) is real, so
\[
V'(t)=2\operatorname{Re}\left(i\int |x|^2\Delta u\,\overline u\,dx\right).
\]
Integrating by parts,
\[
\int |x|^2\Delta u\,\overline u\,dx=-2\int x\cdot\nabla u\,\overline u\,dx-\int |x|^2|\nabla u|^2\,dx,
\]
and the last term is real, giving
\[
V'(t)=4\operatorname{Im}\int x\cdot\nabla u\,\overline u\,dx.
\]
Differentiating again and substituting \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\), \(\overline{u_t}=-i\Delta\overline u-i|u|^{4/d}\overline u\), one separates linear and nonlinear contributions. A standard integration-by-parts computation yields the linear contribution \(8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx\). For the nonlinear contribution, one rewrites the real part as a divergence term and integrates by parts using \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\), obtaining
\[
-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]
Hence
\[
V''(t)=8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]
Comparing with
\[
16E(u)=8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx,
\]
one gets \(V''(t)=16E(u(t))\).

nodes.title: Weighted mass-gradient inequality
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Using \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\),
\[
\|f\|_{L^2}^2=\int |f|^2\,dx=\frac{1}{d}\int (\operatorname{div}x)|f|^2\,dx=-\frac{1}{d}\int x\cdot \nabla(|f|^2)\,dx.
\]
Since \(\nabla(|f|^2)=2\operatorname{Re}(\nabla f\,\overline f)\), this becomes
\[
\|f\|_{L^2}^2=-\frac{2}{d}\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla f\,\overline f\,dx.
\]
Applying \(|\operatorname{Re} z|\le |z|\) and then Cauchy-Schwarz gives
\[
\|f\|_{L^2}^2\le \frac{2}{d}\,\|xf\|_{L^2}\,\|\nabla f\|_{L^2}.
\]

nodes.title: Concavity of the variance forces finite vanishing time
nodes.informal_statement: If \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\) satisfies \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), then \(V\) is a concave quadratic with negative leading coefficient, so there exists a finite forward time \(T\) such that \(V(T)=0\) and \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Energy conservation turns the virial identity into \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), a negative constant. Therefore \(V\) is a quadratic function of \(t\) with negative leading coefficient, hence it tends to \(-\infty\) as \(t\to +\infty\). But by definition \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\ge 0\) on its interval of existence. So \(V\) cannot remain nonnegative for all large positive times. By continuity there must be a finite first forward time \(T\) at which \(V(T)=0\), and consequently \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\).

nodes.title: Variance collapse implies gradient blow-up
nodes.informal_statement:
Let \(M=\|u_0\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}=\|u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\). If \(V(t)=\|xu(t)\|_{L^2}^2\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\), then
\[
\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty,
\]
and therefore \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Apply the weighted inequality to \(f=u(t)\):
\[
\|u(t)\|_{L^2}^2\le \frac{2}{d}\|xu(t)\|_{L^2}\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}.
\]
By mass conservation, \(\|u(t)\|_{L^2}=M>0\), so
\[
M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}.
\]
As \(t\uparrow T\), the factor \(\sqrt{V(t)}\to 0\) while \(M\) stays fixed and positive, forcing \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\to\infty\). Since
\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^1}^2=\|u(t)\|_{L^2}^2+\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}^2
\]
and the \(L^2\) term is constant, it follows that \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}\to\infty\).

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The blow-up argument uses the virial identity \(V''(t)=16E(u(t))\).

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: Finite-time vanishing of the variance comes from the negativity of the constant second derivative.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The final \(H^1\) blow-up conclusion is obtained from variance collapse and the weighted estimate.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The concavity statement uses \(V''(t)=16E(u(t))\) together with energy conservation to get \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The lower bound relating mass, variance, and gradient comes from the weighted inequality.
